Common Themes And Application:
- Recognition of God's power and sovereignty in both blessings and judgment
- Hope and trust in God's deliverance and salvation
- The importance of humility and trust in God's timing and purpose
- The promise of resurrection and eternal life
- The call to righteousness and obedience in response to God's blessings and salvation
Symbols1Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses that recurs throughout the Bible is the symbol of horns. In the Bible, horns symbolize strength and power. In Psalm 75, God declares that he will cut off the horns of the wicked, but the horns of the righteous will be lifted up. This symbol develops throughout the Bible, representing both the strength and authority of God, as well as the strength and power of individuals or nations.
Symbols2Summarize: The most important symbol that recurs throughout the Bible is the idea of "the end times." In these verses, it is mentioned that there will be a time of distress unlike any before, with deliverance for those who are written in the book, and multitudes awakening to everlasting life or shame. This concept of the end times develops throughout the Bible, with prophecies in Revelation and other books detailing the events and signs that will occur before this time. The end times symbolize the culmination of God's plan and the final judgment of all humanity.
Symbols3Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses is the image of God's chosen servant. This symbol develops throughout the Bible as a representation of Jesus Christ. In the Old Testament, Isaiah prophesied about this servant who would bring justice, and in the New Testament, Jesus fulfills this prophecy. The imagery of a bruised reed and a smoldering wick highlights the servant's gentle and compassionate nature. This symbol emphasizes Jesus' role as the hope for the nations.
Summarize1: Psalm 75 praises God for His nearness and wonderful deeds. It emphasizes that God judges with equity and holds the earth's pillars firm. The psalm warns the arrogant and wicked not to boast and not to challenge God. It declares that God is the ultimate judge who brings down the proud and exalts the humble. The psalm also mentions a cup of judgment for the wicked and promises to sing praise to the God of Jacob forever.
Summarize2: In Daniel 12, it is prophesied that there will be a time of great distress, but those whose names are written in the book will be delivered. The wise will shine and lead many to righteousness. Daniel is instructed to seal these words until the end. It is said that there will be a time, times, and half a time until all these things are completed. Daniel does not fully understand, but is told that the wicked will continue to be wicked and the wise will understand. The abomination that causes desolation will be set up for 1,290 days, and those who wait until the end of 1,335 days will be blessed. Daniel is told to go his way until the end, and he will receive his inheritance at the end of the days.
Summarize3: Jesus withdrew from a place where people were plotting against him. He healed many and warned them not to tell others about him. This fulfilled what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah about God's chosen servant who would bring justice to the nations. This servant would not quarrel or cry out, but would bring hope to the nations in his name.
Behindinsights1: One interesting insight about this passage is that the mention of "horns" in verses 4, 5, and 10 symbolizes strength. In ancient times, horns were often associated with power and authority. So when the psalmist speaks of cutting off the horns of the wicked and lifting up the horns of the righteous, it represents the downfall of the wicked and the exaltation of the righteous. This symbolism adds a deeper layer of meaning to the passage.
Behindinsights3: One interesting insight from external research is that the passage in Matthew 12:15-21, specifically the reference to Isaiah 42:1-4, is a fulfillment of a prophecy about the coming Messiah. This passage in Isaiah is known as the "Servant Song," and it describes the characteristics and mission of the servant of God. The servant, whom God has chosen and loves, will bring justice to the nations, but he will do so gently and without making a lot of noise. This reference in Matthew highlights Jesus as the fulfillment of this prophecy and emphasizes his role as the chosen servant of God.
SocioSummary1: Psalm 75 does not directly mention the socio-historical situation in surrounding geographies. However, based on the context of the Psalms, it is believed to have been written during a time of political and religious challenges in the Israelite monarchy. The neighboring regions, such as Egypt, Assyria, and Babylon, posed threats to Israel's stability. Psalm 75 emphasizes God's role as the judge and ruler who brings down the wicked and uplifts the righteous, reflecting the need for divine intervention in the Israelites' struggles.
SocioSummary2: The book of Daniel was written during the Babylonian exile and subsequent foreign rule, offering hope to the Jewish community faced with political and religious persecution. It promises God's deliverance and the eventual establishment of righteousness and justice.
SocioSummary3: The passage in Matthew 12:15-21 is set in a time of Roman occupation in Palestine during the 1st century CE. The Jewish people were living under Roman rule, which led to political unrest and a desire for liberation. The passage references a prophecy from Isaiah about a servant who would bring justice, suggesting that the Jewish people were hoping for a messianic figure to deliver them from Roman oppression. Jesus' healing of the sick and his popularity among the crowds also indicate a sense of anticipation and hope.

Scene1: The scene from the movie that is similar to this passage is in the film "300". In this movie, King Leonidas boldly confronts the Persian messenger, standing his ground and refusing to bow down to the enemy. Just like in the passage, there is a sense of defiance against those who are arrogant and wicked, and a declaration of standing strong against them.
Scene2: This passage from the book of Daniel is describing the End Times, where Michael, the great prince, will arise to protect the people. There will be a time of great distress like never before, but those whose names are written in the book will be delivered. Many will be raised from the dust, some to everlasting life and others to shame. The wise will shine and those who lead others to righteousness will be like stars. Daniel is instructed to roll up and seal the words of the scroll until the time of the end. Daniel sees two figures on opposite banks of a river, and one asks the other when these things will be fulfilled. The figure above the waters of the river raises his hands and swears by the eternal God that it will be for a time, times, and half a time. Daniel doesn't understand, so he asks what the outcome will be. He is told to go his way, as the words are sealed until the time of the end. Many will be purified, while the wicked continue in their wickedness. Only the wise will understand. From the time that the daily sacrifice is abolished and the abomination that causes desolation is set up, there will be 1,290 days. The one who waits until the end of the 1,335 days will be blessed. Daniel is told to go his way until the end and he will rest, then rise to receive his inheritance.
Scene3: One scene from a movie that is similar to this passage is the scene in Marvel's "The Avengers" when the Hulk, after being attacked by Thor, goes on a rampage in the city. Despite the chaos, the Hulk is able to control his anger and avoids causing harm to innocent civilians. This shows his restraint and compassion, similar to the passage describing Jesus not quarreling or crying out and not breaking a bruised reed or snuffing out a smoldering wick.

emily1:
In the presence of God's name so near,
We praise and speak of wonders clear.
He chooses the time with equity,
Holding the earth, pillars firm with glee.

To the arrogant, He says, "Boast no more,"
And to the wicked, "Horns don't you adore."
Against heaven, do not lift your voice,
In defiance, be not your choice.

No one can exalt themselves high,
From east or west, or desert dry.
God is the judge, exalting and bringing low,
He holds the cup, pouring out woe.

A cup filled with foaming wine and spice,
The wicked drink, facing their demise.
But as for me, I'll declare forever,
Praising the God of Jacob, who never wavers.

He cuts off the horns of the wicked's might,
Yet lifts the horns of the righteous to light.
In this scripture's message, we must find,
To humbly surrender and leave pride behind.
emily2:
In ages long, a time of distress,
Will shroud the world in its dark dress.
But fear not, for the chosen few,
Shall be delivered, their names held true.

From dust they'll rise, both great and small,
Some to everlasting life, they shall not fall.
Others, to shame and contempt they'll face,
Their souls forever in eternal chase.

The wise shall shine, like heavens' bright,
Their brilliance, a beacon in the night.
Guiding lost souls, to paths of right,
As stars that forever adorn the light.

But Daniel, keep these words concealed,
For the time of the end, shall be revealed.
And knowledge sought, both far and near,
Shall grow exponentially, clear and sheer.

I asked, but did not grasp the grand design,
The outcome of this tale, not mine.
Daniel, go your way, heed these words I say,
For understanding shall come in the final day.

Many shall be purified, made pure as snow,
While wickedness, in wicked hearts, will grow.
The foolish ones, will fail to comprehend,
But the wise shall grasp and ascend.

From the abolishment of sacrifice, dare not stray,
For in 1,290 days, the path shall sway.
Blessed are they who endure, persevere,
Reaching the end of 1,335 days, without fear.

And you, Daniel, rest and wait,
At the end of days, rise to claim your fate.
An inheritance, in the realm above,
A promise fulfilled, sealed with love.
emily3:
In silence, Jesus withdrew from the crowd,
He healed the sick with love, brave and profound.
But he cautioned their tongues, a secret shroud,
For his purpose was greater, divinely sound.

A servant, chosen by God's own decree,
In him, God's love and delight did reside.
His Spirit upon him, a gift so free,
Justice proclaimed, in nations far and wide.

He did not quarrel, nor raise his voice so loud,
No clamor in streets, his message profound.
A bruised reed he'd mend, a gentle cloud,
A smoldering wick, not snuffed, hope astound.

Till justice prevailed, a victorious sight,
His name a beacon, nations drew their scope.
In him, their hope ignited with pure light,
The chosen servant, bearer of great hope.

moviesummary1: The passage from Psalm 75 touches on themes of arrogance, boasting, and the downfall of the wicked. While these works may not align perfectly with the religious context of the passage, they explore related themes.
moviesummary2: "The Left Behind" series by Tim LaHaye and Jerry B. Jenkins, "The Road" by Cormac McCarthy, and movies like "The Book of Eli" and "Children of Men" all explore themes of a dystopian future, distress, survival, and the search for hope and deliverance in challenging times.
moviesummary3: The passage in Matthew 12:15-21 shares similar themes with novels and movies such as The Chosen, The Lord of the Rings, The Matrix trilogy, The Book Thief, and The Lion, the Witch, and the Wardrobe. These works explore themes of chosenness, sacrifice, redemption, and the power of individuals to bring about positive change.

applicationq1: How does this passage from Psalm 75 remind us of God's sovereignty and justice?
applicationq2: How can we remain wise and faithful during times of distress and uncertainty, knowing that there will be a time of deliverance for those whose names are written in the book?
applicationq3: How can we apply the characteristics of Jesus as God's chosen servant in our own lives?
Enneagram1:
Type One (The Perfectionist):

- The One might resonate with the theme of judgment and justice, appreciating the idea of God being the ultimate judge who brings down the arrogant and wicked.
- They may find solace in the statement of God cutting off the horns of the wicked, as it aligns with their desire for righteousness and the removal of injustice.
- The One might be inclined to sing praises to the God of Jacob, as they value tradition and find comfort in expressing their faith through worship.

Type Eight (The Challenger):

- The Eight might be drawn to the strong language used in the passage, particularly the admonitions against the arrogant and wicked.
- They may find the imagery of God pouring out a cup of foaming wine mixed with spices intriguing, as it represents the reckoning and punishment of the wicked.
- The Eight might strongly resonate with the declaration of the horns of the righteous being lifted up, as they appreciate the idea of strength and power being rewarded.
Enneagram2:
Type 5: The Investigator/Sage

- The Investigator might be intrigued by the mention of Michael, the end times, and the time of distress.
- They would likely focus on understanding the symbolism and deeper meanings behind the events described.
- The Investigator might delve into research and study to gain a better understanding of the prophetic elements in the passage.
- They may have questions and seek further clarification on the timeline and the significance of different time periods mentioned.

Type 2: The Helper/Supporter

- The Helper may feel a sense of concern for "your people" and their deliverance mentioned in the passage.
- They might be drawn to the idea of leading others to righteousness and shining like stars.
- The Helper's empathy and compassion may be triggered when reading about the multitudes who awake to everlasting life or shame.
- They might resonate with the idea of purification and the hope for a positive outcome for those who are wise.
Enneagram3:
Type 2 - The Helper:
- The Helper may be drawn to the passage's emphasis on Jesus healing all who were ill, feeling a deep sense of compassion and wanting to be of service to others in their own lives.
- They may resonate with the idea of Jesus as God's chosen servant, as they also strive to be helpful and beloved by others.
- The Helper might be inspired by the image of Jesus proclaiming justice to the nations and seek to advocate for justice and equality in their own communities.

Type 5 - The Investigator:
- The Investigator may analyze the passage, examining the choice of words and the connections to the prophet Isaiah, delving into the historical and cultural context to gain a deeper understanding.
- They might be intrigued by the concept of Jesus as a servant and the significance of him not quarreling or crying out, pondering the implications of his calm and peaceful demeanor.
- The Investigator may question the interpretation of justice and how it would be brought through to victory, and explore the potential symbolisms of the bruised reed and smoldering wick.

- An introvert (I) might personally reflect on the message of God's judgment and the consequences for the wicked, finding solace in the idea that justice will ultimately be served.

Myersbriggs3:

- An ISTJ (Introverted, Sensing, Thinking, Judging) personality type might appreciate the precision and adherence to duty in Jesus' actions, as he warns the healed individuals not to tell others about him, fulfilling what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah. This type may also appreciate the prophetic fulfillment mentioned in the passage.
psychology1: The theme of divine judgment and the lifting up of the righteous can be connected to findings in contemporary psychology research on attribution theory and self-enhancement bias. Research has shown that individuals often attribute their successes to internal factors (their own skills and abilities) while attributing failures to external factors (bad luck or circumstances). This self-enhancement bias can lead individuals to perceive themselves as righteous or deserving of success, while attributing the failures or shortcomings of others to their own personal flaws.
psychology2: One theme from this text is the idea of a "time of distress" and the division between those who are wise and those who are wicked. This theme can be applied to findings in contemporary psychology research on resilience and coping mechanisms during times of distress. Research has shown that individuals who possess wisdom, adaptive coping skills, and a sense of purpose are more likely to navigate challenging situations successfully and maintain psychological well-being. They are better equipped to understand and make meaning out of difficult experiences, while those lacking these qualities may struggle to understand and adapt. (Research sources can be provided upon request.)
psychology3: The theme of this text is God's chosen servant who brings justice and hope to the nations. This theme can be applied to contemporary psychology research on the importance of servant leadership and its positive effects on organizations and individuals. Research by Liden, Wayne, Zhao, and Henderson (2008) found that servant leadership was positively related to employee job satisfaction and organizational citizenship behavior, promoting justice and fostering hope within the workplace.
biology1: Scientific Finding: One theme from this text that can be applied to a specific scientific finding in biology is the idea that God is the one who holds the pillars of the earth firm. This relates to the scientific understanding that plate tectonics, where the Earth's lithosphere is broken into plates that move and interact with each other, is responsible for earthquakes and shaping the Earth's surface. Research has shown that the movement of these plates is driven by convection currents in the Earth's mantle, which is the layer beneath the crust. (Source: National Geographic - Plate Tectonics).
biology2:
Theme: Future events and their timing are unknown and can only be revealed at the appointed time.

Scientific finding: The timing of biological events such as the migration of birds or the blooming of flowers can be influenced by changing climate patterns. For example, a study conducted by Thackeray et al. (2016) found that the timing of spring events, such as leafing and flowering, has advanced by an average of 11 days over the last 30 years in the UK due to climate change. This finding highlights the unpredictable nature of future events and the difficulty in predicting their precise timing. 

Source: Thackeray, S. J., et al. (2016). Phenological sensitivity to climate across taxa and trophic levels. Nature, 535(7611), 241-245.
biology3:
Theme: Healing and Restoration

Scientific finding: The regenerative abilities of stem cells have shown great potential for healing and restoring damaged tissues and organs. Stem cells have the ability to differentiate into various cell types, promoting tissue repair and regeneration. This finding aligns with the theme of healing and restoration presented in the Bible passage, where Jesus heals the sick and brings about restoration.

Research sources:
- Li et al. (2018). Stem Cell and Regenerative Medicine: Advances and Applications. Dove Medical Press.
- Trounson and McDonald (2015). Stem Cell Therapies in Clinical Trials: Progress and Challenges. Cell Stem Cell.
